Exposes the inputs of the node group. You can have multiple of these nodes in your tree to keep it clean, bringing in each input right where you need it (rather than dragging long links all across your graph).

The input slots can be edited in the Group tab of the Sidebar .

Receives the outputs of the node group. You can have multiple of these nodes in your tree to keep it clean, outputting each result right where it’s produced (rather than dragging long links all across your graph).

The output slots can be edited in the Group tab of the Sidebar .

This section lists all the node groups, both those in the current blend-file and those Linked or Appended from another blend-file.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve information about an image
nodes.description: The Image Info node gets information from image and animation. This can be useful to generate parameters in the geometry node for arbitrary images. Image information can be either general or frame-specific.

inputs.name: Image
inputs.description: Source image to get parameters from.

inputs.name: Frame
inputs.description: Frame index for frame-specific outputs.

outputs.name: Width
outputs.description: The number of pixels along the X axis. Specific to each frame.

outputs.name: Height
outputs.description: The number of pixels along the Y axis. Specific to each frame.

outputs.name: Has Alpha
outputs.description: Whether the transparency channel be different from 1 for the pixels of this image frame. Specific to each frame.

outputs.name: Frame Count
outputs.description: The number of frames in an image or video frame sequence. For a static image, always 1.

outputs.name: FPS
outputs.description: The number of frames per second. For static image is always 0.

nodes.bl_description: Sample values from an image texture
nodes.description:
The Image Texture node is used to add an image file as a texture. The image data is sampled with the input Vector and outputs a Color and Alpha value.

Image Texture displacing a plane. ¶

Notes: Note | Unlike the other texture nodes, this node operates differently in geometry nodes compared to the equivalent shader node. When not connected the Vector input has an implicit position attribute value.

inputs.name: Image
inputs.description: The image socket can be used to connect to the Group Input node. If this is not connected the image can be opened or selected from the node.

inputs.name: Vector
inputs.description: Texture coordinate for texture look-up. If this socket is left unconnected, the Position attribute is used.

inputs.name: Frame
inputs.description: If the Image supports animation, the frame can be set here. This can be keyframed so that the image changes between frames.

outputs.name: Color
outputs.description: RGBA color from the image.

outputs.name: Alpha
outputs.description: Alpha channel from image.

nodes.bl_description: Import geometry from an CSV file
nodes.description:
The Import CSV node reads data from a CSV (Comma-Separated Values) file and generates a point cloud. Each row in the file becomes a point, with numeric columns imported as attributes.

Attribute names are taken from the header row (if present), and types are inferred from the first value in each column:

Integer values create integer attributes.

Float values create float attributes.

This node is useful for visualizing external datasets in Geometry Nodes, such as for scientific visualization or procedural generation based on tabular data.

Notes: Note | Only integer and float columns are supported. Other types, such as strings, are ignored.

inputs.name: Path
inputs.description: The path to the CSV file. The file must be structured with one row per point and values separated by a delimiter.

inputs.name: Delimiter
inputs.description: The character used to separate values within each row of the CSV file, such as a comma ( , ), semicolon ( ; ), or tab character. Tip Tab characters can be inserted using the Special Characters Node .

outputs.name: Point Cloud
outputs.description: A point cloud geometry where each row of the CSV file corresponds to a point.

nodes.bl_description: Import geometry from an OBJ file
nodes.description:
The Import OBJ node loads geometry data from a .obj file and outputs it as instances.

Each object defined in the OBJ file is imported as a separate instance, which helps to preserve scene structure and optimize performance. This approach allows for efficient manipulation and reuse of the geometry within Geometry Nodes workflows.

This node is useful for importing static 3D assets created in other modeling software.

See also

OBJ Import Operator

Notes: Note | The node imports only mesh geometry; materials and textures are not imported.

inputs.name: Path
inputs.description: The path to the .obj file to be imported.

outputs.name: Instances
outputs.description: A collection of instances representing the objects in the imported file.

nodes.bl_description: Import a point cloud from a PLY file
nodes.description:
The Import PLY node imports mesh data from a .ply (Polygon File Format or Stanford Triangle Format) file.

PLY files typically store geometry from 3D scanning or modeling software and can include vertex attributes such as colors or normals. The geometry is imported as a single mesh.

This node allows procedural workflows to incorporate external static geometry directly into Geometry Nodes.

See also

PLY Import Operator

inputs.name: Path
inputs.description: The path to the .ply file to be imported.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: The mesh geometry imported from the PLY file.

nodes.bl_description: Import a mesh from an STL file
nodes.description:
The Import STL node imports mesh geometry from a .stl (Stereolithography) file.

STL files are commonly used for 3D printing and CAD applications and contain only surface geometry, typically as a collection of triangles with no color, texture, or other attributes.

This node enables incorporating external STL assets into Geometry Nodes for procedural processing or visualization.

See also

STL Import Operator

inputs.name: Path
inputs.description: The path to the .stl file to be imported.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: The mesh geometry imported from the STL file.

nodes.bl_description: Import a string from a text file
nodes.description:
The Import Text node reads the contents of a plain text file and outputs it as a single string. This can be useful in motion graphics or data-driven workflows where external text needs to be processed or displayed.

Notes: Note | Currently, only files with the .txt extension are supported.

inputs.name: Path
inputs.description: The path to the txt file.

outputs.name: String
outputs.description: The entire contents of the text file as a single string.

nodes.bl_description: Import volume data from a .vdb file
nodes.description:
The Import VDB node loads volumetric data from a .vdb file and outputs it as a Volume geometry. All grids present in the file are loaded and included in the resulting volume.

This node is useful for importing simulation data or procedural volumes created in external software.

inputs.name: Path
inputs.description: The path to the VDB file to be imported.

outputs.name: Volume
outputs.description: A Volume geometry containing all the grids from the specified VDB file.

nodes.bl_description: Find the nearest element in a group. Similar to the "Sample Nearest" node
nodes.description:
The Index of Nearest node is a way to find other close elements in the same geometry. If needed you can use Group ID to determine the group of neighbors to be analyzed together.

This is an alternative to the Sample Nearest Node node. The main difference is that this node does not require a geometry input, because the geometry from the field context is used.

Tips: Tip | This is often combined with the Evaluate at Index Node or the Sample Index Node node.

inputs.name: Position
inputs.description: The position for each element to search. By default, this is the same as if the Position Node was connected.

inputs.name: Group ID
inputs.description: ID to group elements together.

outputs.name: Index
outputs.description: The index of the closest element in the same geometry component.

outputs.name: Has Neighbor
outputs.description: This is true when the group of the element has at least two elements. This is only relevant when using Group ID .

nodes.bl_description: Choose between an arbitrary number of values with an index
nodes.description:
The Index Switch node outputs one of its inputs based on an integer Index value. Only the selected input is evaluated, making this node useful for switching between multiple data inputs efficiently.

See also

The Menu Switch node provides similar functionality, but exposes the selection as a user-friendly menu rather than an index.

Tips: Tip | When the Index input is connected to a Menu Switch node set to Integer Type , the corresponding menu labels will automatically be shown next to the index value. This provides a clearer context for what each numeric index represents, making node networks more readable.

inputs.name: Index
inputs.description: Determines which of the following input sockets is passed through to the output. The index is zero-based (i.e., the first input corresponds to index 0).

outputs.name: Output
outputs.description: Outputs the value from the input corresponding to the current Index value, without any modification. If the Index value is outside the range of available inputs, the output defaults to the first input or a zero-equivalent value, depending on the data type.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the scene's active camera
nodes.description: The Active Camera node outputs the scene’s current active camera.

outputs.name: Active Camera
outputs.description: Current active camera.

nodes.bl_description: Output a single collection
nodes.description:
The Collection input node outputs a single collection. It can be connected to other collection sockets to make using the same collection in multiple places more convenient.

A reference to the selected Collection.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the position of each Bézier control point's handles
nodes.description:
Gets the two handle positions of each control point in a Bézier spline.

You can use the Set Handle Positions Node to change these positions.

inputs.name: Relative
inputs.description: Output the handle positions relative to the control point instead of in the local space of the geometry.

outputs.name: Left
outputs.description: The position of the control point’s left handle.

outputs.name: Right
outputs.description: The position of the control point’s right handle.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the angle at each control point used to twist the curve's normal around its tangent
nodes.description:
The Curve Tilt node outputs the angle used to turn the curve normal around the direction of the curve tangent in its evaluated points. Keep in mind that the output is per control point, just like the values that can be controlled in curve Edit Mode. For NURBS and Bézier splines, the values will be interpolated to the final evaluated points.

The input node for this data is the Set Curve Tilt node.

outputs.name: Tilt
outputs.description: The tilt angle for the normal in radians.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve whether each edge is marked for smooth or split normals
nodes.description:
The Is Edge Smooth node outputs true for each edge of the mesh that is not marked as sharp. Otherwise, if the edge is marked as sharp, then the node outputs false.

See also

Mark Sharp & Clear Sharp

outputs.name: Smooth
outputs.description: Boolean value that indicates whether the edges of the mesh are not marked as sharp.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve a stable random identifier value from the "id" attribute on the point domain, or the index if the attribute does not exist
nodes.description:
The ID node gives an integer value indicating the stable random identifier of each element on the point domain, which is stored in the id attribute.

The node to set this data is the Set ID Node node.

Notes: Note | Unlike other built-in attributes, the id attribute does not always exist. In that case, this node will output the index .

outputs.name: ID
outputs.description: Integer value.

nodes.bl_description: Input an image data-block
nodes.description:
Image node. ¶

The Image node provides access to a image file which allows you to conveniently enter and switch images for multiple nodes in the tree.

See also

Image Info Node

outputs.name: Image
outputs.description: The image file chosen from the data-block selector.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve an integer value indicating the position of each element in the list, starting at zero
nodes.description:
The Index node gives an integer value indicating the position of each element in the list, starting at zero. This depends on the internal order of the data in the geometry, which is not necessarily visible in the 3D Viewport. However, the index value is visible in the left-most column in the Spreadsheet Editor .

Notes: Note | Indices in geometry data are often defined by the internals of complex algorithms that create it. If no inputs change, indices will be the same when the same node tree is executed multiple times. However, they may not be predictable when inputs to nodes that generate geometry or change its topology are adjusted. Additionally, updates to algorithms in newer versions of Blender may change the order of generated elements. | To avoid relying on consistent indices, it is recommended to calculate them locally, or to avoid operations that change topology when they must be consistent over time.

outputs.name: Index
outputs.description: Integer value which enumerates each point on the geometry.

nodes.bl_description: Calculate position bounds of each instance's geometry set
nodes.description:
The Instance Bounds node outputs the axis-aligned bounding box of each instance in the input geometry. This can be used to determine the spatial extent of instances, for example, to position or scale objects based on their size.

Notes: Note | Only top-level instances are considered; the bounds do not include nested instances inside the instance geometry. This avoids the performance cost of realizing nested instances for accurate bounds. | See Nested Instancing for more information.

inputs.name: Use Radius
inputs.description: For curves, point clouds, and Grease Pencil geometries, take the radius attribute into account when computing the bounds.

outputs.name: Min
outputs.description: The minimum corner of the bounding box for each instance, in local space.

outputs.name: Max
outputs.description: The maximum corner of the bounding box for each instance, in local space.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the rotation of each instance in the geometry
nodes.description:
The Instance Rotation outputs the XYZ Euler rotation of each top-level instance in the local space of the modifier object.

The Instances page contains more information about geometry instances.

Notes: Note | Though rotations are often displayed in units of degrees in the spreadsheet or node editor, they are stored internally in radians, so this node outputs radians.

outputs.name: Rotation
outputs.description: Vector that indicates the rotation of each top-level instance in radians.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the scale of each instance in the geometry
nodes.description:
The Instance Scale outputs the size of top-level instances on each axis in the local space of the modifier object.

The Instances page contains more information about geometry instances.

outputs.name: Scale
outputs.description: Vector that indicates the scale of each top-level instance.

nodes.bl_description: Output a single material
nodes.description:
The Material input node outputs a single material. It can be connected to other material sockets to make using the same material name in multiple places more convenient.

A reference to the selected material.

Tips: Tip | The Material node can also be added by dragging and dropping a material data-block into the node editor. This will add the node and select the dropped material in the Data-Block Menu .

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the index of the material used for each element in the geometry's list of materials
nodes.description:
The Material Index node outputs which material in the list of materials of the geometry each element corresponds to. Currently the node supports mesh data, where material_index is a built-in attribute on faces.

The node to set this data is the Set Material Index node.

outputs.name: Material Index
outputs.description: Standard integer value, with a minimum value of zero.

nodes.bl_description: The angle between the normals of connected manifold faces
nodes.description:
The Edge Angle node calculates the angle in radians between two faces that meet at an edge. For the Face, Face Corner, and Point domains , the node uses simple domain interpolation to move values from the mesh’s edges.

Notes: Note | The output of this node depends on the density of the mesh. If there are more edges closer together and the curvature of the mesh stays the same, the edge angle will be different.

outputs.name: Unsigned Angle
outputs.description: The shortest angle in radians between two faces where they meet at an edge. The range of the data is from zero to PI. Flat edges and Non-manifold edges have an angle of zero. An edge between two faces completely folded back on each other has an angle of PI, or 180 degrees. Tip Computing this value is slightly faster than the signed angle, so if there is no need to distinguish between convex and concave angles, using this value can provide a performance improvement.

outputs.name: Signed Angle
outputs.description: The signed angle in radians between two faces where they meet at an edge. Flat edges and Non-manifold edges have an angle of zero. Concave angles are positive and convex angles are negative.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the number of faces that use each edge as one of their sides
nodes.description:
The Edge Neighbors node outputs topology information relating to each edge of a mesh.

Using the Face Count output to create a curve on a mesh’s boundary edges. ¶

outputs.name: Face Count
outputs.description: The number of faces that use the edge as one of their sides. When the value is one, the edge is a non-manifold boundary edge. Alternatively, when the value is zero, the edge is a loose edge, not used by any faces.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve topology information relating to each edge of a mesh
nodes.description:
The Edge Vertices node outputs the position and index of the two vertices of each of a mesh’s edges.

Notes: Note | The order of the two vertices of an edge is arbitrary. In some cases it may be predictable based on the internals of the algorithm that created the mesh, but in general the order should not be relied upon.

nodes.bl_description: Calculate the surface area of a mesh's faces
nodes.description:
The Face Area node outputs the surface area of a mesh’s faces. The units are in Blender units no matter the unit system, equivalent to meters-squared at the default unit scale.

Combined with the Attribute Statistic Node , this node can be used to calculate the total surface area of a mesh.

Notes: Note | For quads and N-gons , when the face’s vertices are not planar, the output is not necessarily the same as the sum of every one of the face’s triangles visible in the viewport. In this case it should only be used an approximation. In some cases, the Triangulate Node can be used to get an exact value.

outputs.name: Area
outputs.description: The surface area of each of the mesh’s faces.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve whether all triangles in a face are on the same plane, i.e. whether they have the same normal
nodes.description:
The Is Face Planar node outputs whether every triangle of a quads or N-gons is on the same plane as all of the others, in other words, if they have the same normal .

For example, a non-planar face can be created by moving a single vertex in a face but not the others. Triangles will always be planar.

Combined with the Set Material Node , this node is used to visualize all non-planar faces in a mesh.

inputs.name: Threshold
inputs.description: The distance a point can be from the surface before the face is no longer considered planar.

outputs.name: Planar
outputs.description: Whether each mesh face is planar.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve topology information relating to each face of a mesh
nodes.description: The Face Neighbors node outputs topology information relating to each face of a mesh.

outputs.name: Vertex Count
outputs.description: This output is simply the number of sides of each face, or how many corners each face has.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve information about separate connected regions in a mesh
nodes.description:
The Mesh Island node outputs information about separate connected regions, or “islands” of a mesh. Whenever two vertices are connected together by an edge, they are considered as part of the same island, and will have the same Island Index output.

This node’s behavior is similar to the Select Linked operator in edit mode, or the Random per Island output of the Geometry shader node .

outputs.name: Island Index
outputs.description: The index of each vertex’s island. Indices are decided based on the lowest vertex index contained in each island.

outputs.name: Island Count
outputs.description: The total number of mesh islands. This is a single value, and does not vary per element.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve topology information relating to each vertex of a mesh
nodes.description: The Vertex Neighbors node outputs topology information relating to each vertex of a mesh.

outputs.name: Vertex Count
outputs.description: The number of vertices connected to this vertex with an edge, equal to the number of connected edges.

outputs.name: Face Count
outputs.description: The number of faces that contain the vertex.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the data of a specified attribute
nodes.description: The Named Attribute node outputs the data of an attribute based on the context of where it is connected (the Field Context ).

inputs.name: Name
inputs.description: The name of the attribute to read.

outputs.name: Attribute
outputs.description: The attribute data stored on the geometry.

outputs.name: Exists
outputs.description: True if the attribute accessed by the node is present in the connected context.

nodes.bl_description: Output a selection of a Grease Pencil layer
nodes.description:
The Named Layer Selection node outputs a Boolean field whose index value is true for Grease Pencil layers whose name matches a string input.

Notes: Note | The specified layer must already exist in the object. | If no matching layer is found, the selection will be false for all elements.

inputs.name: Name
inputs.description: The name of the Grease Pencil layer to match.

outputs.name: Selection
outputs.description: A Boolean field for which the index for layers whose name matches the input string will be true; all others are false.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve a unit length vector indicating the direction pointing away from the geometry at each element
nodes.description:
The Normal node returns a vector for each evaluated point indicating the normal direction . The output can depend on the attribute domain used in the node evaluating the field, but the output is always a normalized unit vector .

On the face domain, the normal is the “up” direction of the face.

For mesh vertices, the normal is an average of the surrounding face normals. If the vertex does not have any connected faces, the output is simply the normalized position of that vertex.

The normal output for each edge is the average of the edge’s two vertex normals.

The output for each face corner is the same as the face normal of the corresponding face.

The output of this node when used for curve geometry is the evaluated normal of the curve, which depends on the twist method. The normal vector is always perpendicular to the direction of the curve’s path at every point.

Warning

For NURBS and Bézier spline curves, keep in mind that the value retrieved from this node is the value at every control point, which may not correspond to the visible evaluated points. For NURBS splines the difference may be even more pronounced and the result may not be as expected. A Resample Curve Node can be used to create a poly spline, where there is a control point for every evaluated point.

outputs.name: Normal
outputs.description: Vector indicating the normal of each geometry element.

outputs.name: True Normal
outputs.description: For meshes, outputs normals without custom normals attributes taken into account.

nodes.bl_description: Output a single object
nodes.description:
The Object input node outputs a single object. It can be connected to other object sockets to make using the same object in multiple places more convenient.

A reference to the selected object.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve a vector indicating the location of each element
nodes.description:
The Position node outputs a vector of each point of the geometry the node is connected to.

The node can work on geometry domains besides points. In that case, the position data will be automatically interpolated to the new domain. For example, when used as part of the input to the Split Edges Node , the position for each edge will be the average position of the edge’s two vertices.

For instances themselves, the output is the origin of each instance. However, if the node is for a geometry node that adjusts data inside instances, the position output of this node will be in the local space of each instance. See the Instance Processing page for more details.

outputs.name: Position
outputs.description: Vector that indicates the location of each element of the geometry.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the radius at each point on curve or point cloud geometry
nodes.description: The Radius node outputs the radius value at each point on the evaluated geometry. For curves, this value is used for things like determining the size of the mesh created in the Curve to Mesh node. For point clouds, the value is used for the display size of the point in the viewport.

outputs.name: Radius
outputs.description: Float value indicating radius at each point on the geometry.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the current time in the scene's animation in units of seconds or frames
nodes.description: The Scene Time node outputs the current time in the scene’s animation in units of seconds or frames.

outputs.name: Seconds
outputs.description: Current scene time in seconds.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve whether each face is marked for smooth or sharp normals
nodes.description: The Is Face Smooth node outputs true for each face of the mesh if that face is marked to render smooth shaded. Otherwise, if the face is marked to render as flat shaded, then the node outputs false.

outputs.name: Smooth
outputs.description: Boolean value that indicates whether the normals of each face corner on the final mesh are smoothed with normal of all adjacent faces or not.

nodes.bl_description: Find the shortest paths along mesh edges to selected end vertices, with customizable cost per edge
nodes.description:
The Shortest Edge Paths node finds paths along mesh edges to a selection of end vertices. The cost used to define “shortest” can be set to anything. By default there is a constant cost for every edge, but a typical input would be the length of each edge.

The output is encoded with vertex indices, and is meant to be used on the vertex domain. For each vertex, the Next Vertex Index output gives the index of the following vertex in the path to the “closest” endpoint.

The node is implemented with Dijkstra’s algorithm .

See also

This node can be used with the Edge Paths to Selection Node or the Edge Paths to Curves Node to generate new geometry based on the paths.

Shortest Edge Paths on a deformed Ico Sphere. ¶

Gradient-colored maze showing shortest paths to a target sphere. ¶

Organic branching structure on Suzanne. ¶

Tips: Tip | The edge length is a natural input to the Edge Cost . It can be implemented with the Edge Vertices Node and the Vector Math Node set to the Distance operation.

inputs.name: End Vertex
inputs.description: A selection of the goal vertices that terminate the edge paths.

inputs.name: Edge Cost
inputs.description: The weight for each edge, used to determine the meaning of “shortest.”

outputs.name: Next Vertex Index
outputs.description: The following vertex on the shortest path from every vertex to the closest endpoint (as defined by the cost input).

outputs.name: Total Cost
outputs.description: The remaining cost before an end vertex is reached.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve whether each spline endpoint connects to the beginning
nodes.description:
The Is Spline Cyclic controls whether each of the curve splines start and endpoints form a connection. Its output corresponds to the built-in cyclic attribute on the curve spline domain.

The node to set this data is the Set Spline Cyclic Node .

outputs.name: Cyclic
outputs.description: Whether the spline is cyclic.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the number of evaluated points that will be generated for every control point on curves
nodes.description:
The Spline Resolution outputs the number of evaluated curve points that will be generated for every control point on the spline. This node works for NURBS, Bézier, and Catmull Rom splines.

For poly splines, there is a one-to-one correspondence between original points and evaluated points, so the resolution does not have an effect.

On Bézier splines, the resolution does not have an effect on segments between vector handles, since there are no extra evaluated points between the neighboring control points.

The node to set this data is the Set Spline Resolution Node .

outputs.name: Resolution
outputs.description: The integer resolution value for each spline.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the direction of curves at each control point
nodes.description:
The Curve Tangent node outputs the direction that a curve points in at each control point, depending on the direction of the curve (which can be controlled with the Reverse Curve Node ). The output values are normalized vectors.

Warning

For NURBS and Bézier spline curves, keep in mind that the value retrieved from this node is the value at every control point, which may not correspond to the visible evaluated points. For example, a Bézier spline might have 48 evaluated points, but only four control points, if its resolution is 12. For NURBS splines the difference may be even more pronounced and the result may not be as expected. A Resample Curve Node can be used to create a poly spline, where there is a control point for every evaluated point.

outputs.name: Tangent
outputs.description: The direction of the curve at every control point.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the integer coordinates of the voxel that the field is evaluated on
nodes.description:
The Voxel Index node outputs the integer index of the voxel that the field is currently being evaluated on.

Unlike a position in object space, the voxel index refers to the discrete cell coordinates inside a voxel grid. This makes it possible to reason about neighboring voxels directly in index space, without converting through world or object space.

This node can be combined with nodes such as Integer Math and Sample Grid Index to sample values from nearby voxels by offsetting the indices.

Whether the current evaluation is happening on a tile rather than on a single voxel.

Some operations evaluate fields on tiles that cover multiple voxels at once for performance reasons. In those cases the coordinates given by X , Y , and Z alone are not enough to describe the full covered region.

If this is false, the extent is always 1.

The number of voxels along each axis of the tile.

For a normal voxel, the extent on each axis is 1 . For a tile that covers multiple voxels, the extents describe how many voxels are grouped together in X, Y, and Z.

These outputs can be used to detect and handle cases where an evaluation represents an aggregated region (a tile) instead of an individual voxel, so that the result is not misinterpreted as referring to just one voxel.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a reference to geometry at each of the input points, without duplicating its underlying data
nodes.description:
The Instance on Points node adds a reference to a geometry to each of the points present in the input geometry. Instances are a fast way to add the same geometry to a scene many times without duplicating the underlying data. The node works on any geometry type with a Point domain, including meshes, point clouds, and curve control points.

Any attributes on the points from the Geometry input will be available on the instance domain of the generated instances.

Notes: Note | To instance object types that do not contain geometry, like a light object, the Object Info Node can be used. Other objects like Metaball objects are not supported for instancing.

Tips: Tip | The Make Instances Real operator can be used to create objects from instances generated with this node.

inputs.name: Points
inputs.description: Standard geometry input. The position of the points of this geometry affect the transforms of each instance output. Note If the input geometry contains instances, the node will create more instances on the points inside the instances, creating nested instancing . In this case, each new instance will have the transform created by the node from the Rotation and Scale inputs, but it will also be transformed based on the parent instances.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: Whether to instance on each point. True values mean an instance will be generated on the point, false values mean the point will be skipped.

inputs.name: Instance
inputs.description: The geometry to instance on each selected point. This can contain real geometry, or multiple instances, which can be useful when combined with the Pick Instance option.

inputs.name: Instance Index
inputs.description: The selection of index for every selected point, only used when Pick Instances is true. By default the point ID is used, or the index if that doesn’t exist. Negative values or values that are too large are wrapped around to the other end of the instance list.

inputs.name: Rotation
inputs.description: The Euler rotation for every instance. This can use the rotation output of nodes like Distribute Points on Faces and Curve to Points . An Euler rotation can also be created from a direction vector like the normal with the Align Rotation to Vector Node .

inputs.name: Scale
inputs.description: The size of each generated instance.

outputs.name: Instances
outputs.description: Standard geometry output. If the id attribute exists on the input geometry, it will be copied to the result instances.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve the full transformation of each instance in the geometry
nodes.description:
The Instance Transform outputs the Transformation Matrix of each top-level instance in the local space of the modifier object.

The Instances page contains more information about geometry instances.

nodes.bl_description:
Generate points at the origins of instances.
Note: Nested instances are not affected by this node
nodes.description:
The Instances to Points node generates points at the origins of top-level instances. Attributes on the instance domain are moved to the point cloud points.

Notes: Note | Top-level instances are those that are owned by the node’s input geometry. Instances owned by other instances, i.e. nested instances, are not considered by this node.

inputs.name: Instances
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: The instances used to generate points. True values mean a point is created for the instance, false values mean the instance is skipped.

inputs.name: Position
inputs.description: Overrides the default position of generated point.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: Controls the radius of the result points.

outputs.name: Points
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.bl_description: Generate new curves on points by interpolating between existing curves
nodes.description: Generate new curves on points by interpolating between existing curves. This is useful to have a smaller set of original curves to make editing easier and faster while still generating high-density curves for the viewport or a final render.

inputs.name: Guide Curves
inputs.description: Base curves that new curves are interpolated between.

inputs.name: Guide Up
inputs.description: Optional up vector that is typically a surface normal. Supplying an up vector will improve the quality of the interpolation, making it aware of the surface shape which it otherwise wouldn’t have a way to know about. Tip In a typical “child hair” generation setup, this up direction is retrieved with a combination of the Sample UV Surface Node using the same geometry that the points were distributed on, and the Normal Node .

inputs.name: Guide Group ID
inputs.description: Splits guides into separate groups. New curves interpolate existing curves from a single group. This input can be useful for generating hair parts by stopping curves in different sections (with different group IDs) from affecting each other.

inputs.name: Points
inputs.description: The positions of the first root control points of the newly generated interpolated curves.

inputs.name: Point Group ID
inputs.description: The curve group to interpolate in.

inputs.name: Max Neighbors
inputs.description: Maximum amount of close guide curves that are taken into account for interpolation.

outputs.name: Curves
outputs.description: The guide curves with interpolated curves.

outputs.name: Closest Index
outputs.description: Index of the closest guide curve for each generated curve. Note Internally this node mixes the data from multiple guide curves, with the maximum number of sources depending on the Max Neighbors input. This output is only the index of the curve with the largest weight.

outputs.name: Closest Weight
outputs.description: Weight of the closest guide curve for each generated curve.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve whether the nodes are being evaluated for the viewport rather than the final render
nodes.description:
The Is Viewport node outputs true when geometry nodes are evaluated for the viewport. For the final render the node outputs false.

Using Is Viewport to switch between low quality and high quality. ¶

outputs.name: Is Viewport
outputs.description: Boolean value that indicates whether geometry nodes are evaluated for preview.

nodes.bl_description: Merge separately generated geometries into a single one
nodes.description:
The Join Geometry node merges separately generated geometries into a single one. If the geometry inputs contain different types of data, the output will also contain different data types.

When multiple mesh inputs contain different materials, the material slots from each mesh geometry are merged so that the output mesh will contain all the input materials.

When merging attributes from multiple geometry inputs, the highest complexity data type is chosen for the output attribute. For example, if a weight attribute is a Boolean on one geometry input and a vector on another, the weight attribute on the output geometry will use the vector data type.

Standard geometry output.

Notes: Note | The node cannot handle the case when more than one geometry input has a volume component. | Note | Vertex groups are preserved when realizing instances or joining geometries. If the domain and type propagation rules above result with the vertex domain and float type, then an attribute will be a vertex group on the output mesh.

inputs.name: Geometry
inputs.description: Geometry that will be joined. Multiple inputs are allowed. When the node is muted, only the first link will be passed through.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve a value from a list
nodes.description: Get List Item node.

nodes.bl_description: Count how many items are in a given list
nodes.description: List Length node.

nodes.bl_description: Provide a selection of faces that use the specified material
nodes.description: The Material Selection node provides a selection for meshes that use this material. Since the material_index is stored on each face, the output will be implicitly interpolated to a different domain when necessary. For example, every vertex connected to a selected face will be selected.

inputs.name: Material
inputs.description: Standard material input.

outputs.name: Selection
outputs.description: Selection of faces that use the input material.

nodes.bl_description: Select from multiple inputs by name
nodes.description:
The Menu Switch node outputs one of its inputs based on a selected menu item. Only the active input is evaluated, allowing efficient switching between multiple options.

The available menu entries are defined by the user. Menu items can be added and removed, as well as renamed and reordered in the editor side bar. Renaming a menu entry keeps existing links of the matching input socket.

The menu can be used in node groups and the nodes modifier UI. Connecting the menu input with a Group Input node will expose the menu as a group input. A menu socket in a node group, reroute node, or other pass-through nodes needs to be connected to a Menu Switch node in order to work. An unconnected menu socket will show an empty menu by default.

Connecting multiple Menu Switch nodes to the same output socket creates a conflict (even when the menu entries are the same). To avoid this a menu switch can be wrapped in a node group. Multiple node groups of the same type can be connected to the same menu, since they contain the same menu switch node.

Conflict caused by connecting different menus. ¶

Same node group can be connected without conflict. ¶

See also

The Index Switch Node is similar but it exposes the choices as an integer index.

inputs.name: Menu
inputs.description: Determines which input socket is passed through to the output.

outputs.name: Output
outputs.description: Outputs the value from the input corresponding to the selected menu item, unchanged.

nodes.bl_description: Merge vertices or points within a given distance
nodes.description:
The Merge by Distance node merges selected mesh vertices or point cloud points within a given distance, merging surrounding geometry where necessary. This operation is similar to the Merge by Distance operator or the Weld Modifier .

Standard geometry output.

Using the selection input to only merge some of the points in a point cloud. ¶

inputs.name: Geometry
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: Boolean field that is true for parts of the geometry to be deleted. Unselected points will be completely unused for the operation– they will not be merged into other points, and no points will merge into them either. Tip When possible, using the selection input can be a simple way to speed up the node, since searching for nearby points is a relatively expensive operation that gets even more expensive when more points are involved.

inputs.name: Distance
inputs.description: The distance to use for searching for nearby points.

nodes.bl_description: Join groups of Grease Pencil layers into one
nodes.description:
Combines multiple Grease Pencil Layers into a single layer.

See also

Merge Layers Operator

inputs.name: Grease Pencil
inputs.description: The Grease Pencil object with multiple layers to combine.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: Operate of a subset of layers by setting the field layer index value to true . By default, all layers are selected.

inputs.name: Group ID
inputs.description: The index numbers of the layer to be merged. This input is only visible when using By Group ID Mode.

outputs.name: Grease Pencil
outputs.description: The Grease Pencil object with combined layers.

nodes.bl_description: Cut, subtract, or join multiple mesh inputs
nodes.description:
The Mesh Boolean Node allows you to cut, subtract, and join the geometry of two inputs. This node offers the same operations as the Boolean modifier .

Standard geometry output.

A boolean attribute field with a selection of the edges that were created where the two inputs meet.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a circular ring of edges
nodes.description: The Mesh Circle node generates a circular ring of edges that is optionally filled with faces.

inputs.name: Vertices
inputs.description: Number of vertices on the circle. No geometry is generated when the number is below three.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: Distance of the vertices from the origin.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a cone mesh
nodes.description:
Generates a cone mesh that is optionally truncated.

Notes: Note | If the top and bottom radii are both zero, this node will output a single line.

inputs.name: Vertices
inputs.description: Number of vertices in the top and/or bottom circle of the cone. No geometry is generated if the number is below three.

inputs.name: Side Segments
inputs.description: Number of vertically stacked face loops that make up the cone’s sides. Increasing this will add horizontal cuts. No geometry is generated if the number is below one.

inputs.name: Fill Segments
inputs.description: Number of concentric rings in the top and/or bottom. No geometry is generated if the number is below one.

inputs.name: Radius Top
inputs.description: The radius of the cone’s top circle. If this is zero, the circle is reduced to a single vertex.

inputs.name: Radius Bottom
inputs.description: Same as Radius Top but for the bottom circle.

inputs.name: Depth
inputs.description: Height of the generated cone.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

outputs.name: Top
outputs.description: A boolean field with a selection of the faces on the top of the cone. If the Fill Type is set to None , this will be a selection of the top edges instead. If Radius Top is zero, this will be a selection of the top vertex.

outputs.name: Bottom
outputs.description: A boolean field with a selection of the faces on the bottom of the cone. If the Fill Type is set to None , this will be a selection of the bottom edges instead. If Radius Bottom is zero, this will be a selection of the bottom vertex.

outputs.name: Side
outputs.description: A boolean field with a selection of the faces on the side of the cone.

outputs.name: UV Map
outputs.description: The default UV coordinate of each face corner. This can be connected to the Store Named Attribute Node for populating a UV Map .

nodes.bl_description: Generate a cuboid mesh with variable side lengths and subdivisions
nodes.description: The Cube node generates a cuboid mesh with variable side lengths and subdivisions. The inside of the mesh is still hollow like a normal cube.

inputs.name: Size
inputs.description: Side lengths along each of the main axes.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

outputs.name: UV Map
outputs.description: A 2D vector representing the default X/Y coordinates of the UV Map for the primitive’s shape. This can be connected to the Store Named Attribute Node , to be used once the Geometry Nodes Modifier get applied. The UV map must be stored on the face corner in order to be accessed.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a cylinder mesh
nodes.description: The Cylinder node generates a cylinder mesh. It is similar to the Cone node but always uses the same radius for the circles at the top and bottom.

inputs.name: Vertices
inputs.description: Number of vertices on the circle at the top and bottom. No geometry is generated if the number is below three.

inputs.name: Side Segments
inputs.description: Number of edges running vertically along the side of the cone. No geometry is generated if the number is below one.

inputs.name: Fill Segments
inputs.description: Number of concentric rings used to fill the round faces at the top and bottom. No geometry is generated if the number is below one.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: Distance of the vertices from the Z axis. If this is zero, the output will be a single line.

inputs.name: Depth
inputs.description: Height of the cylinder.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

outputs.name: Top
outputs.description: A boolean attribute field with a selection of the faces on the top of the cylinder. If the Fill Type property is None , then this will be a selection of the top edges instead. If the Radius is zero, this will be a selection of the top point.

outputs.name: Side
outputs.description: A boolean attribute field with a selection of the faces on the side of the cylinder.

outputs.name: Bottom
outputs.description: This is the same as the Top selection output, but on the bottom side of the geometry instead.

outputs.name: UV Map
outputs.description: A 2D vector representing the default X/Y coordinates of the UV Map for the primitive’s shape. This can be connected to the Store Named Attribute Node , to be used once the Geometry Nodes Modifier get applied. The UV map must be stored on the face corner in order to be accessed.

nodes.bl_description: Find edges on the boundaries between groups of faces with the same ID value
nodes.description:
The Face Group Boundaries Node finds the edges which lie on the boundaries of specified regions. These edges could be used to mark seams for UV unwrapping, for example.

Selection of the boundary edges of the different face sets. An edge is considered to be at the boundary if it lies on at least two faces with different identifiers.

Combined with the UV Unwrap Node , this node is used to turn the face sets (right cube) into a UV map for a texture (left cube).

inputs.name: Face Group ID
inputs.description: Identifier for which group of faces this face belongs to. All contiguous faces with the same value are in the same region.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a planar mesh on the XY plane
nodes.description: The Grid node generates a planar mesh on the XY plane.

inputs.name: Size X
inputs.description: Side length of the plane in the X direction.

inputs.name: Size Y
inputs.description: Side length of the plane in the Y direction.

inputs.name: Vertices X
inputs.description: Number of vertices in the X direction. If this is smaller than two, no mesh is generated.

inputs.name: Vertices Y
inputs.description: Number of vertices in the Y direction. If this is smaller than two, no mesh is generated.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

outputs.name: UV Map
outputs.description: A 2D vector representing the default X/Y coordinates of the UV Map for the primitive’s shape. This can be connected to the Store Named Attribute Node , to be used once the Geometry Nodes Modifier get applied. The UV map must be stored on the face corner in order to be accessed.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a spherical mesh that consists of equally sized triangles
nodes.description: The Icosphere node generates a spherical mesh that consists of equally sized triangles.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: Distance of the vertices from the origin.

inputs.name: Subdivisions
inputs.description: Number of subdivisions on top of the most basic icosphere. The number of faces quadruple with every subdivision.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

outputs.name: UV Map
outputs.description: A 2D vector representing the default X/Y coordinates of the UV Map for the primitive’s shape. This can be connected to the Store Named Attribute Node , to be used once the Geometry Nodes Modifier get applied. The UV map must be stored on the face corner in order to be accessed.

nodes.bl_description: Generate vertices in a line and connect them with edges
nodes.description: The Mesh Line node generates vertices in a line and connects them with edges.

inputs.name: Count
inputs.description: Number of vertices on the line.

inputs.name: Resolution
inputs.description: Length of individual edges. The node tries to fit as many vertices as possible between the start and end point. The exact end point might not be hit. This is only available when the mode is set to End Points and the count mode is set to Resolution .

inputs.name: Start Location
inputs.description: Position of the first vertex.

inputs.name: Offset
inputs.description: Controls the direction of the line and distance between the vertices. This is only available when the mode is set to Offset .

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a curve from a mesh
nodes.description:
The Mesh to Curve node converts a mesh into one or more curve splines.

Two different conversion modes are supported, depending on the desired output:

Edges : Turns each string of connected mesh edges into a poly spline. Whenever two or more edge strings intersect, they will be split into separate splines.

Faces : Creates a cyclic spline from each mesh face. This mode is generally much faster than Edges , as it parallelizes easily and can share face and corner attributes without needing to copy them.

Loose vertices are ignored – they will not be turned into single-point splines.

Attributes, both named and unnamed ones, are transferred to the resulting splines. If there is a radius attribute, it will be applied as such, although you may find it more convenient to use the Set Curve Radius Node for this.

inputs.name: Mesh
inputs.description: Standard mesh input.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: A field input evaluated on the edge domain to determine whether each edge will be included in the result. Tip Using this input is more efficient than deleting parts of the geometry before or after the conversion.

outputs.name: Curve
outputs.description: Generated curve.

nodes.bl_description: Create a filled volume grid from a mesh
nodes.description:
The Mesh to Density Grid node converts a mesh into a density grid , where each voxel stores a scalar value representing how far it lies inside or near the surface of the mesh. This can be used to generate fog volumes, soft-body representations, or as input to volumetric and field-based effects.

The resulting grid contains smooth gradients that transition from high values inside the mesh to low values outside, allowing for continuous blending and sampling operations.

inputs.name: Mesh
inputs.description: The input mesh to be converted into a density grid. The mesh defines the surface that will be voxelized.

inputs.name: Density
inputs.description: The scalar value assigned to voxels inside the mesh. Typically a value of 1.0 for full density and 0.0 for empty space, though any scalar range can be used to represent material thickness or opacity.

inputs.name: Voxel Size
inputs.description: The size of each voxel in object space units. Smaller values produce higher-resolution grids with more detail, while larger values reduce detail but improve performance and memory efficiency.

inputs.name: Gradient Width
inputs.description: The width of the gradient region inside the mesh. Controls how smoothly the density falls off from the interior toward the exterior. Larger values create a broader transition, while smaller values produce a sharper surface boundary.

outputs.name: Density Grid
outputs.description: The resulting float grid representing the density of the mesh. Voxels inside the mesh will have higher density values, while voxels outside the mesh will approach zero. The smooth transition is defined by the Gradient Width parameter.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a point cloud from a mesh's vertices
nodes.description: The Mesh to Points node generates a point cloud from a mesh.

inputs.name: Mesh
inputs.description: Standard Mesh input.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: The meshes used to generate a point cloud.

inputs.name: Position
inputs.description: Positions of generated points. By default, this input is the same as if the Position Node was connected.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: Radii of generated points.

outputs.name: Points
outputs.description: Generated point cloud.

nodes.bl_description: Create a signed distance volume grid from a mesh
nodes.description:
The Mesh to SDF Grid node converts a mesh into a Signed Distance Field (SDF) grid. Each voxel in the resulting grid stores the shortest distance to the surface of the mesh, with the sign indicating whether the voxel is inside or outside the mesh.

Positive values represent distances outside the mesh, negative values represent distances inside the mesh, and zero corresponds to the mesh surface. SDF grids are useful for many applications, including surface reconstruction, collision detection, morphing, and volumetric modeling.

inputs.name: Mesh
inputs.description: The input mesh whose volume is converted into a signed distance field grid.

inputs.name: Voxel Size
inputs.description: The size of each voxel in object space units. Smaller values produce higher-resolution fields that more accurately capture surface details, but require more memory and computation.

inputs.name: Band Width
inputs.description: The width of the active voxel band around the mesh surface, measured in voxels. Only voxels within this band store meaningful signed distance values. Increasing this value captures a wider region around the surface at the cost of additional memory.

outputs.name: SDF Grid
outputs.description: A float grid representing the signed distance field of the mesh. Each voxel contains the shortest signed distance to the nearest surface point of the mesh, with negative values inside and positive values outside.

nodes.bl_description: Create a fog volume with the shape of the input mesh's surface
nodes.description: The Mesh to Volume node creates a fog volumes based on the shape of a mesh. The volume is created with a grid of the name "density" .

inputs.name: Mesh
inputs.description: Standard Mesh input.

inputs.name: Density
inputs.description: Value of voxels inside the generated fog volume.

inputs.name: Interior Band Width
inputs.description: The maximum distance of the included voxels to the surface on the inside of the mesh.

outputs.name: Volume
outputs.description: The generated volume grid.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a spherical mesh with quads, except for triangles at the top and bottom
nodes.description: The UV Sphere node generates a spherical mesh mostly out of quads except for triangles at the top and bottom.

inputs.name: Segments
inputs.description: Horizontal resolution of the sphere. If this is smaller than three, no mesh is generated.

inputs.name: Rings
inputs.description: Vertical resolution of the sphere. If this is smaller than two, no mesh is generated.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: Distance of vertices to the origin.

outputs.name: Mesh
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

outputs.name: UV Map
outputs.description: A 2D vector representing the default X/Y coordinates of the UV Map for the primitive’s shape. This can be connected to the Store Named Attribute Node , to be used once the Geometry Nodes Modifier get applied. The UV map must be stored on the face corner in order to be accessed.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve information from an object
nodes.description:
The Object Info node gets information from objects. This can be useful to control parameters in the geometry node tree with an external object, either directly by using its geometry, or via its transformation properties.

An Object Info node can be added quickly by dragging an object into the node editor.

inputs.name: Object
inputs.description: Object to get the properties from.

inputs.name: As Instance
inputs.description: Output the entire object as single instance instead of realized geometry. This allows instancing non-geometry object types, because the output will contain an instance of the object.

outputs.name: Transform
outputs.description: Transformation Matrix containing the location, rotation and scale of the object.

outputs.name: Location
outputs.description: Location of the object in world space.

outputs.name: Rotation
outputs.description: Rotation of the object in world space.

outputs.name: Scale
outputs.description: Scale of the object in world space.

outputs.name: Geometry
outputs.description: Geometry of the object in world space with all its modifiers applied.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve corners in the same face as another
nodes.description:
Retrieves another corner in the same face as the input corner. This is like “rotating” the input corner around in its face.

Conceptually the operation is similar to the Offset Point in Curve Node .

inputs.name: Corner Index
inputs.description: The index of the input face corner. Note If this input is not connected, it uses the index of the context item, which means it’s important that the node is evaluated in the Face Corner domain.

inputs.name: Offset
inputs.description: The number of corners to move around the face before finding the result, circling back to the first corner if necessary.

outputs.name: Corner Index
outputs.description: The index of the offset face corner.

nodes.bl_description: Offset a control point index within its curve
nodes.description:
The Offset Point in Curve node retrieves other points in the same curve as the input control point. This is like starting at a specific control point and walking along neighboring points toward the start or end of the curve.

Conceptually the operation is similar to the Offset Corner in Face Node , but the point index doesn’t wrap around to the other end of the curve unless it is cyclic.

inputs.name: Point Index
inputs.description: The index of the input control point. Note By default this uses the index from the field context, which makes it important that the node is evaluated on the point domain.

inputs.name: Offset
inputs.description: The number of points to move around the curve before finding the result. If the curve is cyclic and the offset goes past the start or end point of the curve, it will wrap around to the other side.

outputs.name: Is Valid Offset
outputs.description: Whether the input control point plus the offset is a valid index of the original curve. Any offset in a cyclic curve is always valid.

outputs.name: Point Index
outputs.description: The index of the offset curve point.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a point cloud with positions and radii defined by fields
nodes.description:
The Points node generate a point cloud with positions and radii defined by fields .

Notes: Note | Since the point cloud is created from scratch, the Position and Radius inputs can only depend on the index node. Regular input nodes like the position won’t work.

inputs.name: Count
inputs.description: The number of points to create.

inputs.name: Position
inputs.description: The position of each generated point.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: The radius of each point.

outputs.name: Points
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.bl_description: Retrieve a point index within a curve
nodes.description: The Points of Curve node retrieves indices of specific control points in a curve.

inputs.name: Curve Index
inputs.description: The index of the input curve. Note By default this uses the index from the field context, which makes it important that the node is evaluated on the curve domain.

inputs.name: Weights
inputs.description: Values used to sort the curve’s control points. By default the control points are sorted by index, so the control points with the smallest indices come first.

inputs.name: Sort Index
inputs.description: Which of the sorted control points to use for the Point Index output. If the value is larger than the total number of control points, it will wrap around to the beginning.

outputs.name: Point Index
outputs.description: The index of one of the curve’s control points, chosen by the Sort Index input.

outputs.name: Total
outputs.description: The number of control points in the curve.

nodes.bl_description: Split all points to curve by its group ID and reorder by weight
nodes.description:
The Points to Curves node generates a Curves geometry by taking all points and inserting them to new curves. All Attributes from points are propagated to Curve Points . Built-in curves attributes stored in points will be ignored.

The above example creates a curve Array with connections between curves. This is created by duplicating the Arc primitive curve with the Duplicate Elements Node . Each curve is shifted in a top direction based on its index value. All the curves are converted to the Point Cloud by the Curve to Points Node . Finally, the points are converted to curves by the Points to Curves node.

All the Points of the resulting Curves geometry have the same attributes as points on the initial Arc primitive.

Notes: Note | If points of curve have the same Weight value, the order will be the same as its original relative location. Without any Weight and Group ID inputs, each point will have the same indices in the curve.

Tips: Tip | To simplify thinking about points, attributes and their positions in each curve, The weight of each point in curve can be associated with a point attributes value. The sorting and grouping will be reflected on the attributes as like on the Weight and Group ID.

inputs.name: Points
inputs.description: The Point Cloud geometry component.

inputs.name: Curve Group ID
inputs.description: All points with the same Group ID value will be joined in the same curve. The value of Group ID can be any value (negative, zero, or infinity, etc.). All created curves must have at least a single point. The order of curves depends both on Group ID value and on the order of Group ID values in the Point Cloud.

inputs.name: Weight
inputs.description: If the curve contains more than one Point, the Weight of each Point is used to define the order of all points in curve via sorting. The goal of sorting is to have points with the minimal Weight value at the start of curve and the maximum Weight at the end of curve.

outputs.name: Curves
outputs.description: The curves with all copied points from the Point Cloud, but joined in curves. All other components aren’t saved. The resulting curves are always non-cyclic.

nodes.bl_description: Create a signed distance volume grid from points
nodes.description:
The Points to SDF Grid node generates a Signed Distance Field (SDF)*grid from a set of input points. Each voxel in the resulting grid stores the shortest signed distance to the nearest point, allowing points to be represented as smooth, volumetric shapes.

Positive values represent distances outside the influence of the points, negative values represent distances inside, and zero corresponds to the surface of the generated implicit sphere around each point.

This node is useful for constructing volumetric fields or collision volumes from particles, instances, or procedurally generated point clouds.

inputs.name: Points
inputs.description: The input points that define the centers of the generated SDF regions.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: The radius of influence for each point, defining how far the signed distance extends from the point center. Each point is treated as a sphere with this radius.

inputs.name: Voxel Size
inputs.description: The size of each voxel in object space units. Smaller voxel sizes yield higher-resolution fields that better capture fine detail, at the cost of increased memory and processing time.

outputs.name: SDF Grid
outputs.description: A scalar (float) grid representing the signed distance field of the input points. Each voxel stores the shortest signed distance to any point’s surface, with negative values inside the combined spheres and positive values outside.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a mesh vertex for each point cloud point
nodes.description: The Points to Vertices node generate a mesh vertex in the output geometry for each point cloud point in the input geometry.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: Boolean field used to determine if each point will be converted to a vertex.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a fog volume sphere around every point
nodes.description:
The Points to Volume node generates a fog volume sphere around every point in the input geometry. The new volume grid is named “density”.

It usually makes sense to combine this node with the Volume to Mesh Node .

Warning

This node expects that point positions are not extremely large. For position values of many billions, the behavior isn’t guaranteed, and it may be unstable.

inputs.name: Points
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Density
inputs.description: Value of voxels inside the generated fog volume.

inputs.name: Radius
inputs.description: Specify the radius of the sphere generated at each point.

outputs.name: Volume
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.bl_description: Compute the closest location on the target geometry
nodes.description:
The Geometry Proximity node computes the closest location on the target geometry.

The different modes of the node: faces, edges and points. In this example the Geometry Nodes modifier is added on the target plane. Note that the larger plane is subdivided and the smaller plane is not.

The three target element modes: faces, edges, and points. ¶

Points distributed on a sphere used as a target for a distance used in a shader. ¶

Tips: Tip | The Map Range Node is often helpful to use with the distance output of this node to create a falloff with a maximum distance.

inputs.name: Geometry
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Group ID
inputs.description: Splits the elements of the input geometry into groups which can be sampled individually.

inputs.name: Sample Position
inputs.description: The given position to calculate the closest location on the target.

inputs.name: Sample Group ID
inputs.description: Determines in which group the closest nearest element is detected.

outputs.name: Position
outputs.description: Closest location on the surface of the target mesh, or the closest point in the target point cloud in Points mode.

outputs.name: Distance
outputs.description: Distance (as floating-point value) from the source position to the closest location in the target.

outputs.name: Is Valid
outputs.description: Whether the sampling was successful. It can fail when the sampled group is empty.

nodes.bl_description: Cast rays from the context geometry onto a target geometry, and retrieve information from each hit point
nodes.description: The Raycast node intersects rays from one geometry onto another. The source geometry is defined by the context of the node that the Raycast node is connected to. Each ray computes hit points on the target mesh and outputs normals, distances and any surface attribute specified.

inputs.name: Target Geometry
inputs.description: Geometry that rays are tested against.

inputs.name: Attribute
inputs.description: An optional field input evaluated on the Target Geometry that will be interpolated at the hit points. The resulting values are outputted with the Attribute output.

inputs.name: Source Position
inputs.description: The position from where to start each ray. By default, this is the same as if the Position Node was connected.

inputs.name: Ray Direction
inputs.description: Direction of each ray from the starting position. The field is evaluated on the geometry from the context of the field evaluation, not the Target Geometry .

inputs.name: Ray Length
inputs.description: Maximum distance a ray can travel before being considered “no hit”.

outputs.name: Is Hit
outputs.description: Boolean output that is true for each ray which has hit the Target Geometry .

outputs.name: Hit Position
outputs.description: The location of the intersection point with the target mesh.

outputs.name: Hit Normal
outputs.description: The surface Normal vector at the hit location.

outputs.name: Hit Distance
outputs.description: The distance from the Source Position to the Hit Position . If the ray does not hit, the Ray Length is returned.

outputs.name: Attribute
outputs.description: Interpolated values of the Attribute input sampled at the Hit Position .

nodes.bl_description: Convert instances into real geometry data
nodes.description:
The Realize Instances node makes any instances (efficient duplicates of the same geometry) into real geometry data. This makes it possible to affect each instance individually, whereas without this node, the exact same changes are applied to every instance of the same geometry. However, performance can become much worse when the input contains many instances of complex geometry, which is a fundamental limitation when procedurally processing geometry.

When merging attributes from multiple geometry inputs, the highest complexity data type is chosen for the output attribute. For example, if a weight attribute is a Boolean on one geometry input and a vector on another, the weight attribute on the output geometry will use the vector data type.

Named and anonymous attributes are propagated from the instance domain to the realized geometry. If an attribute exists both on the base geometry and on an instance, the attribute values from the base geometry take precedence.

Notes: Note | If the input contains multiple volume instances, only the first volume component is moved to the output. | Note | The id attribute receives special handling to prevent duplicate values. id values or indices of each instance are combined with id values from the geometry data points. | Vertex groups are preserved when realizing instances or joining geometries. If the domain and type propagation rules above result with the vertex domain and float type, then an attribute will be a vertex group on the output mesh.

inputs.name: Geometry
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: Which top-level instances to realize.

inputs.name: Realize All
inputs.description: Realize all levels of nested instances for each top-level instances (overrides the value of the Depth input).

inputs.name: Depth
inputs.description: Number of levels of nested instances to realize for each top-level instance.

outputs.name: Geometry
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.bl_description: Delete an attribute with a specified name from a geometry. Typically used to optimize performance
nodes.description:
The Remove Named Attribute node deletes an attribute with a certain name from its geometry input. Any attribute that exists on geometry data will be automatically propagated when the geometry storing it is changed, which can be an expensive operation, so using this node can be a simple way to optimize the performance of a geometry node tree or even to lower the memory usage of the entire scene.

Almost all named attributes can be removed. For certain Built-In Attributes , removing it will mean that a default value will be used instead. For example, removing the cyclic attribute on curves means that all curves will be non-cyclic.

inputs.name: Geometry
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Name
inputs.description: The name of the attribute to remove.

outputs.name: Geometry
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.description:
A Repeat Zone executes a set of nodes multiple times.

The zone consists of an input node on the left, an output node on the right, and an orange area in the middle for placing the nodes to repeat. When the zone executes for the first time, it evaluates its inputs and forwards them to its inner nodes. These inner nodes can then write to the output node for providing inputs to the next iteration, and for providing the result of the zone after the last iteration.

The nodes inside the zone can also take inputs from nodes outside the zone – these inputs are then the same in every iteration. However, nodes inside the zone can’t send their outputs to nodes outside the zone.

The above example generates a pyramid with a configurable number of levels. Each iteration creates a cube, brings it into position, and joins it with the result of the previous iteration.

inputs.name: Iterations
inputs.description: Number of times to execute the zone. The Iteration socket gives the index of the current iteration, starting from 0.

nodes.description:
A Repeat Zone executes a set of nodes multiple times.

The zone consists of an input node on the left, an output node on the right, and an orange area in the middle for placing the nodes to repeat. When the zone executes for the first time, it evaluates its inputs and forwards them to its inner nodes. These inner nodes can then write to the output node for providing inputs to the next iteration, and for providing the result of the zone after the last iteration.

The nodes inside the zone can also take inputs from nodes outside the zone – these inputs are then the same in every iteration. However, nodes inside the zone can’t send their outputs to nodes outside the zone.

The above example generates a pyramid with a configurable number of levels. Each iteration creates a cube, brings it into position, and joins it with the result of the previous iteration.

inputs.name: Geometry
inputs.description: Default geometry input. Further inputs can be added by connecting a node’s output to the zone’s blank input, or by using the Repeat Items list in the node’s Properties panel. The inputs can be renamed by clicking them with Ctrl - LMB in the zone itself or in the Repeat Items list. The latter also accepts double clicking.

nodes.bl_description: Swap one material with another
nodes.description:
The Replace Material node swaps one material with another. Replacing a material with this node is more efficient than creating a selection of all faces with the old material with the Material Selection Node and then using the Set Material Node .

Notes: Note | Currently this node only adjusts mesh data.

inputs.name: Geometry
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Old
inputs.description: Material that is going to be replaced.

inputs.name: New
inputs.description: Material that is replacing the old material.

outputs.name: Geometry
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.bl_description: Generate a poly spline for each input spline
nodes.description:
The Resample Curve node creates a poly spline for each input spline. In the Count and Length modes, the control points of the new poly splines will have uniform spacing.

Tips: Tip | Use a field as an input to have a different count/length for each spline.

inputs.name: Curve
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Selection
inputs.description: Whether or not to resample each spline. True values mean spline will be resampled to a poly spline, false values mean the spline will be unaffected.

outputs.name: Curve
outputs.description: Standard geometry output.

nodes.bl_description: Change the direction of curves by swapping their start and end data
nodes.description:
The Reverse Curve node swaps the start and end of splines. The shape of the splines is not changed.

Tips: Tip | When used on the Profile input of the Curve to Mesh Node , this node fill flip the normals of the resulting mesh.

inputs.name: Curve
inputs.description: Standard geometry input.

inputs.name: Selection
